Periodic acid ($\mathrm{HIO}_{4}$) cleaves the carbon-carbon bonds between adjacent carbons bearing hydroxyl groups or a hydroxyl and a carbonyl group.

In one molecule of glucose, there are five such carbon-carbon bonds (C1-C2, C2-C3, C3-C4, C4-C5, and C5-C6).

Cleavage of each bond consumes one mole of $\mathrm{HIO}_{4}$.

Thus, 5 moles of $\mathrm{HIO}_{4}$ are consumed per mole of glucose, producing 5 moles of formic acid ($\mathrm{HCOOH}$) and 1 mole of formaldehyde ($\mathrm{HCHO}$).

Answer: 5

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following classifications does NOT apply to glucose?
questions.solution:
Glucose is a simple sugar with the molecular formula $C_6H_{12}O_6$.

It cannot be hydrolyzed further to give simpler units of polyhydroxy aldehyde or ketone, so it is classified as a monosaccharide.

Oligosaccharides are carbohydrates that yield two to ten monosaccharide units on hydrolysis.

Glucose contains an aldehyde group, making it an aldose, and it has six carbon atoms, making it a hexose.

Therefore, glucose is not an oligosaccharide.

Answer: Oligosaccharide

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the cyclic structure of D-fructofuranose, which carbon atom is the anomeric carbon?
questions.solution:
D-fructose is a ketohexose with the ketone group located at the C-2 position in its open-chain structure.

During the formation of the cyclic D-fructofuranose structure, the hydroxyl group at C-5 undergoes an intramolecular nucleophilic addition to the carbonyl carbon at C-2, forming a hemiketal ring.

The carbonyl carbon (C-2) becomes a new chiral center in this cyclic structure. This newly formed chiral center is known as the anomeric carbon.

Therefore, the anomeric carbon in D-fructofuranose is C-2.

Answer: C-2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The cyclic hemiacetal shown below is in equilibrium with an acyclic open-chain compound $\mathrm{X}$ in aqueous solution.


Identify the structure of compound $\mathrm{X}$.
questions.solution:
The given compound is a six-membered cyclic hemiacetal.

In aqueous solution, it undergoes ring opening to form an acyclic aldehyde.

The anomeric carbon ($\text{C}_1$), which is bonded to both the ring oxygen and a hydroxyl group, becomes the aldehyde carbonyl carbon ($-\text{CHO}$) upon ring opening.

The ring oxygen is attached to $\text{C}_5$ of the chain. When the $\text{C}_1-\text{O}$ bond breaks, this oxygen becomes a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) on $\text{C}_5$.

The substituent on $\text{C}_5$ is a $-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$ group, which constitutes $\text{C}_6$.

Therefore, the open-chain form has a six-carbon skeleton with an aldehyde at $\text{C}_1$ and hydroxyl groups at $\text{C}_5$ and $\text{C}_6$.

The structure is $\text{HO}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO}$ (5,6-dihydroxyhexanal).

Option (A) correctly shows this 5,6-dihydroxyhexanal structure.

Options (B), (C), and (D) represent a ketone, a triol, and a carboxylic acid, respectively, which cannot form the given hemiacetal.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements regarding the given structures is true?

(i)

(ii)

(iii)
options: Structures (i) and (ii) are epimers, and structures (i) and (iii) are epimers.
All three structures are epimers of each other.
questions.solution:
Epimers are diastereomers that differ in configuration at exactly one stereocenter.

Let us compare the given structures:
Structure (i) is D-glucose.
Structure (ii) is D-galactose.
Structure (iii) is D-mannose.

Comparing structure (i) and structure (ii):
They have the same configuration at C2, C3, and C5, but differ in configuration only at C4. Therefore, structures (i) and (ii) are C4 epimers.

Comparing structure (i) and structure (iii):
They have the same configuration at C3, C4, and C5, but differ in configuration only at C2. Therefore, structures (i) and (iii) are C2 epimers.

Comparing structure (ii) and structure (iii):
They differ in configuration at two stereocenters (C2 and C4). Thus, they are not epimers.

Hence, structures (i) and (ii) are epimers, and structures (i) and (iii) are epimers.

Answer: Structures (i) and (ii) are epimers, and structures (i) and (iii) are epimers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:

$\xrightarrow{\text{(i) } \mathrm{MeOH} / \mathrm{H}^+} \xrightarrow{\text{(ii) } \mathrm{HIO}_4} \xrightarrow{\text{(iii) } \mathrm{NaBH}_4}$ ?
questions.solution:
The given starting material is D-erythrose, an aldotetrose.

Step (i): Reaction with $\mathrm{MeOH} / \mathrm{H}^+$
D-erythrose undergoes intramolecular cyclization where the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group at C4 attacks the aldehyde group at C1 to form a 5-membered hemiacetal ring (erythrofuranose). In the presence of acidic methanol, the anomeric hydroxyl group is converted to a methoxy group, forming methyl erythrofuranoside. The ring consists of an oxygen atom bonded to C1 and C4. C1 is bonded to $-\mathrm{OMe}$, C2 and C3 have $-\mathrm{OH}$ groups, and C4 is a $-\mathrm{CH}_2-$ group.

Step (ii): Reaction with $\mathrm{HIO}_4$
Periodic acid ($\mathrm{HIO}_4$) cleaves vicinal diols. In methyl erythrofuranoside, the vicinal diol is present between C2 and C3. Cleavage of the C2-C3 bond converts both C2 and C3 into aldehyde ($-\mathrm{CHO}$) groups.
The C1 fragment becomes $\mathrm{OHC}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OMe})-$ and the C4 fragment becomes $-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CHO}$. Since both are still connected via the ring oxygen, the resulting intermediate is a dialdehyde: $\mathrm{OHC}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OMe})-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CHO}$.

Step (iii): Reaction with $\mathrm{NaBH}_4$
Sodium borohydride ($\mathrm{NaBH}_4$) reduces the aldehyde groups to primary alcohols ($-\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{OH}$).
The dialdehyde $\mathrm{OHC}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OMe})-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CHO}$ is reduced to $\mathrm{HOCH}_2-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OMe})-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{OH}$.

This structure corresponds exactly to the molecule shown in Option (A).
